synthesis:
The most useful “gut check” idea this week: we’re getting better at *knowing when our procedures are lying to us*—whether that’s privacy noise quietly breaking your hypothesis test, a bandit policy inflating false positives, or an ML posterior that looks confident because it’s under-trained. A common theme across these papers is turning fuzzy reliability concerns into something you can actually measure: power curves, coverage, calibration error, disclosure-risk bounds.

### Privacy that you can reason about like a test plan

Fondeville et al. give a surprisingly practical reframing of differential privacy: treat membership inference as a hypothesis test, then interpret your privacy parameters as controlling an adversary’s best ROC curve. The nice new knob is **relative disclosure risk**—how much an attacker can multiplicatively update their belief after seeing the DP output.

Two takeaways for practitioners:

If you’re using **pure ε-DP**, you automatically get bounded relative disclosure risk (ρ ≤ e^ε). That’s the cleanest “safety case” statement in the batch.

Mechanism choice matters in a way we don’t always say out loud: **Laplace noise gives bounded disclosure risk; Gaussian noise gives unbounded ρ** (yet still avoids “blatant disclosure”). In other words, Gaussian can fail “gracefully,” but the worst-case belief update can still be arbitrarily large.

They also do something QE folks will appreciate: translate privacy noise into **test power loss** (worked example: Z-test on a private mean). Under their illustrative settings, strong privacy doesn’t necessarily torch your power once n is moderately sized.

### Adaptive experiments: stop trusting your p-values

Li et al. tackle the nasty, common situation where we run a multi-armed bandit to learn faster… and then report a classical t-test/ANOVA as if the data were i.i.d. Spoiler: Type I error inflates.

Their fix—**Algorithm-Induced Test (AIT) correction**—is conceptually simple: simulate your *exact same bandit algorithm* under a null world, then calibrate the critical value of your usual test statistic from that Monte Carlo null distribution. You keep the familiar test (t, ANOVA, Tukey), but you stop pretending the sampling plan didn’t matter.

The practical punchline: naive fixed thresholds produced false positive rates as high as ~0.13 in their settings; AIT brought it back to ~0.05. Even better, when they optimize the bandit design using an “experiment extension cost” tradeoff, they find ε-Thompson variants that can beat both uniform randomization and vanilla TS for a given power/cost target.

If you run adaptive A/Bs in manufacturing ops, clinical-ish monitoring, or materials discovery: this is one of the more “ready to try” ideas this week.

### Reliability isn’t accuracy: uncertainty methods that behave differently in the real world

Ruijs et al. compare **MC Dropout** (Bayesian-ish uncertainty) vs **Inductive Conformal Prediction (ICP)** on CNNs. The result feels familiar but useful to see quantified:

A higher-accuracy model (their VGG16 variant) can still be *more overconfident* and poorly calibrated.

MC Dropout improved calibration a lot for GoogLeNet (ECE roughly halved), barely at all for VGG16.

ICP hits ~95% empirical coverage for both models, but the *prediction set sizes* expose ambiguity (the “Shirt” class explodes into larger sets).

If you need “reliability envelopes” for vision models on the shop floor, ICP’s coverage guarantee (under exchangeability) is a compelling baseline—even when Bayesian approximations don’t calibrate.

### Degradation + telemetry budgets: active learning for satellite electronics

Li et al. bring a very QE-flavored contribution: a hierarchical Wiener degradation model with nonlinear time transformation and stress covariates, plus **spatial correlation between neighboring components**. The key is the **two-stage active learning**: choose *which units* to sample (space-filling) and *when* to sample (penalized D-optimality).

The headline is data-efficiency: they report comparable or better reliability prediction accuracy with far fewer measurements—and show how assuming independence can bias you badly even if you monitor *more* units.

### Simulation-based inference: making posteriors earn your trust

Delaunoy’s dissertation is a sober reminder: neural SBI can give posteriors that look crisp and are simply overconfident. The proposed diagnostics center on **expected coverage** (simulation-based calibration-style), and the methodological push is “balancing” regularization (BNRE) plus Bayesian neural nets with functional priors to represent approximation uncertainty in low-simulation regimes.

No silver bullets, but a clear operational message: if you’re using SBI for reliability physics models or rare-event simulators, **coverage checks should be part of your verification plan**, not an afterthought.

### DOE trend: batch design is becoming “distributional” and scalable

Two BOED papers point in the same direction: batch experimental design is moving beyond greedy coordinate exchange.

Bhattacharya et al. show that approximating EIG via a Gaussian/D-optimal surrogate and learning a **surrogate Fisher information** can make high-dimensional (even image-based) designs tractable—and materially shrink credible intervals for hard-to-identify memory parameters.

Sharrock et al. take a more theoretical route: lift batch designs to **probability measures**, add entropic regularization, and optimize via **Wasserstein gradient flows** with particle methods. The benefit is better exploration of multimodal utilities—exactly where standard gradient ascent tends to get stuck.

### Question to sit with this week

We’re increasingly good at *procedural guardrails*—coverage for SBI, calibrated tests for bandits, bounded disclosure risk for DP. What’s the analogous guardrail you wish you had for your own reliability pipeline: a single diagnostic that tells you when the whole workflow is quietly violating its assumptions?

papers.title: Balancing the privacy-utility trade-off: How to draw reliable conclusions from private data
Optimal Experimental Design for Reliable Learning of History-Dependent Constitutive Laws
Wasserstein Gradient Flows for Batch Bayesian Optimal Experimental Design
A Statistically Reliable Optimization Framework for Bandit Experiments in Scientific Discovery
papers.authors: Tong Li|Travis Mandel|Goldie Phillips|Anna Rafferty|Eric M. Schwartz|Dehan Kong|Joseph J. Williams

papers.title: Beyond Accuracy: Reliability and Uncertainty Estimation in Convolutional Neural Networks
Adaptive Active Learning for Online Reliability Prediction of Satellite Electronics
papers.authors: Shixiang Li|Yubin Tian|Dianpeng Wang|Piao Chen|Mengying Ren
